title: Good company
pros: Good work, good food, good office
cons: No such con that i can think of

title: Moving at the speed of light, burn out is inevitable
pros:
1) Food, food, food.  15+ cafes on main campus (MTV) alone.  Mini-kitchens, snacks, drinks, free breakfast/lunch/dinner, all day, errr'day.

2) Benefits/perks.  Free 24:7 gym access (on MTV campus).  Free (self service) laundry (washer/dryer) available.  Bowling alley.  Volley ball pit.  Custom-built and exclusive employee use only outdoor sport park (MTV).  Free health/fitness assessments.  Dog-friendly.  Etc. etc. etc.

3) Compensation.  In ~2010 or 2011, Google updated its compensation packages so that they were more competitive.  

4) For the size of the organization (30K+), it has remained relatively innovative, nimble, and fast-paced and open with communication but, that is definitely changing (for the worse).

5) With so many departments, focus areas, and products, *in theory*, you should have plenty of opportunity to grow your career (horizontally or vertically).  In practice, not true.  

6)  You get to work with some of the brightest, most innovative and hard-working/diligent minds in the industry.  There's a "con" to that, too (see below).
cons:
1) Work/life balance.  What balance?  All those perks and benefits are an illusion.  They keep you at work and they help you to be more productive.  I've never met anybody at Google who actually time off on weekends or on vacations.  You may not hear management say, "You have to work on weekends/vacations" but, they set the culture by doing so - and it inevitably trickles down.  I don't know if Google inadvertently hires the work-a-holics or if they create work-a-holics in us.  Regardless, I have seen way too many of the following: marriages fall apart, colleagues choosing work and projects over family, colleagues getting physically sick and ill because of stress, colleagues crying while at work because of the stress, colleagues shooting out emails at midnight, 1am, 2am, 3am.  It is absolutely ridiculous and something needs to change.

2) Poor management.  I think the issue is that, a majority of people love Google because they get to work on interesting technical problems - and these are the people that see little value in learning how to develop emotional intelligence.  Perhaps they enjoy technical problems because people are too "difficult."  People are promoted into management positions - not because they actually know how to lead/manage, but because they happen to be smart or because there is no other path to grow into.  So there is a layer of intelligent individuals who are horrible managers and leaders.  Yet, there is no value system to actually do anything about that because "emotional intelligence" or "adaptive leadership" are not taken seriously.

3) Jerks.  Sure, there are a lot of brilliant people - but, sadly, there are also a lot of jerks (and, many times, they are one and the same).  Years ago, that wasn't the case.  I don't know if the pool of candidates is getting smaller, or maybe all the folks with great personalities cashed out and left, or maybe people are getting burned out and it's wearing on their personality and patience.  I've heard sto
advice_to_management:
1) Don't dismiss emotional intelligence and adaptive leadership.  They're not just catch phases.  You need great managers and leaders in order to build great companies and develop great employees.  The people who may be brilliant at solving technical issues may not be (and are most often, not) the best candidates for management. 

2) Do something about that work-ife balance.  Don't just have a bunch of pow-wows and tech talks and discussions about it.  Leadership should actually model it.  Consider re-evaluating how work is done; what processes are in place that are inefficient and ineffective and need to be updated or removed?  

3) Don't forget that there is already a pool of incredibly talented people within the company.  If career development is really a goal at Google, then do it.  Don't just hire from the outside.  Take the time to help your employees develop their careers - then maybe you won't lose some of the great ones, and maybe you'll have prevent some of that burn out and disillusionment.

title: Great balance between big-company security and fun, fast-moving projects
pros:
* If you're a software engineer, you're among the kings of the hill at Google. It's an engineer-driven company without a doubt (that *is* changing, but it's still very engineer-focused).
* The perks are amazing. Yes, free breakfast, lunch, an dinner every weekday. Aaaaaamazing holiday parties (at Waldorf Astoria, NY Public Library, MoMA, etc.); overnight ski trips to Vermont; overnight nature trips to the Poconos in the summer; summer picnics at Chelsea piers; and on and on and on. I don't see this going away unless the company starts hurting financially.
* Speaking of which, the company is doing quite well, which reflects in bonuses and equity grants.
* There a huge diversity of work ranging from defending independent journalism worldwide (Google Project Shield) to crisis response during disasters (see Maps during Hurricane Sandy or Tsunamis), to the best machine learning experts and projects in the world, to more mundane revenue-driving projects in advertising, there's really something for everybody.
* It's easy to move around within the company as long as you're in good standing (the vast majority of engineers are).
* The company is amazingly open: every week Larry Page and Sergey Brin host what's called TGIF where food, beer, wine, etc. is served, a new project is presented, and afterward there's an open forum to ask the executives anything you want. It's truly fair game to ask anything, no matter how controversial, and frequently the executives will be responsive.
* No, nobody cares if you use an iPhone, Facebook, shop with Amazon, stream using Spotify, or refuse to use Google+. The company is amazingly open and flexible.

Neither pro nor con, but general information on work-life balance, promotions, and advancement.
* Work life balance can be what you want it to be on most teams. (Some teams are in more competitive sectors and require more crazy hours all the time - but very few of them). If you do what's expected, you'll be fine at least for a handfu
cons:
* It *is* becoming larger, and with it comes growing pains: bureaucracy, slow to respond to market threats, bloated teams, cross-divisional tension (though nothing remotely approaching that of Microsoft's internal tension).
* The quality of the engineers is possibly dropping, but possibly not. It's hard to get real metrics, because as the absolute number of people grows, naturally the number of bad apples grows; as a percentage it's supposedly the same as it ever was, but with larger numbers of poorer quality engineers it just _feels_ like things might be changing for the worse.
* Also with growth means more internal-confidential data leaks (again, because of the raw numbers of people) -- product announcements being ruined, etc. That means the company has to be tighter-lipped internally to avoid leaks, which makes things less open. It's still an amazingly open place, but less so than it was even a couple years ago. The good thing is they recognize it and actively look to improve things because they know how important it is to keep the good culture.
advice_to_management: Keep the focus on the user. Everything else will follow.

title: The best place I've worked and also the most demanding.
pros:
You can't find a more well-regarded company that actually deserves the hype it gets.

You'll work on cutting edge projects / solve important issues that impact your community and the world

You'll meet interesting people who are your colleagues, managers, and senior management.

You'll open the paper and see your company in the news almost every day, and read about projects you're working on, which is a cool thing

You'll see Larry and Sergey at TGIF and you'll admire how they lead the company. They are brilliant, goofy, low key but intense, and likeable. 

There are 22 cafes (more or less), the food is excellent, and it's free.

Your pay will typically be competitive, though it needs to be tweaked up a bit since the economy has improved.

Google cares about how it treats its employees.

The campus is like an academic campus in many ways.

There are tons of activities on campus, like authors speaking about their books, films after work, and gyms where you can work out - but you'll need to make sure to carve out time to do these things.

You'll get plenty of external validation from people who suddenly think you're smart and rich because you work there, even if you're not rich and you're as smart when you didn't work at Google.

If and when you leave, you'll never regret having that company on your resume. It opens doors. 

The company is flexible - if you're lucky, you won't have a micromanager boss and you can be somewhat flexible in how you work - but don't get me wrong - you'll work a LOT. But you don't have to do all of it chained to your desk.
cons:
I live in SF so the commute can take between 1.5 hours to 1.75 hours each way on the shuttle - sometimes 2 hours each way on a busy day or rainy day. That means being on the bus for 3-4 hours PER DAY. It's a wired bus though which means you can work on the way to Mountain View. But it can feel brutal.

Your first year or two are really important in terms of your career at Google and they affect how you're viewed, and your ability to be promoted. You should always ask to work on high profile projects. If you don't get them, don't expect to get high ratings or get promoted. Always volunteer for cross functional group work for maximum exposure, and then work hard at those things.

You'll likely work on something that no one will explain to you and it will take you at least a year to be comfortable doing what you're working on, even if you're super quick at learning.  No one has time to train you or teach you what you're doing - which is kind of hard.

After two or three years, people you started out with at Google start to get promoted. If you're not one of them, you'll wonder why and how it happened, and that process is somewhat political and not always clear. 

It's a big company now. And super political. So don't be naive. Expect some people to be catty, some people to be territorial, and be prepared to be mentally tough. Don't let people see your vulnerableness. It's a Darwinistic culture with a huge dose of 30-something idealism on top which can fool you into thinking that people are easygoing - they're not. They're driven. If you're not driven, you're not going to fit in.

When you start at Google, it seems like peer reviews are super important - they are, but they are the sprinkles on the fro yo. The important thing is that your direct manager knows your work, likes your work and likes you, and then you can get promoted. If your boss doesn't like you, all the positive peer reviews in the world won't help you.  Make sure you know what your boss wants, a
advice_to_management: Keep on NOT micromanaging - that is a huge benefit to Google. Most of us have a huge workload and we work in spikes and not chained to our desks, and we care deeply about producing, and we produce a lot. We can do that because you respect us enough to give us some freedom in how we do our jobs.

title: Great place to start out, lots of room to grow
pros: Lots of talented and smart people, great benefits, lots of room to grow and learn from others. Kinda easy to coast but there's also lots of pushing to grow and see results. Great place to advance, learn a lot, and be around smart people. Also really nice buildings and great food.
cons:
Leadership is increasingly perceived as not listening to typical rank-and-file employees. Quiet layoffs throughout the past few years (a team here, some managers there, a re-org causing a few people to be laid off, etc.) has put a damper on morale.

I was also laid off which is disappointing since my team and position in Google Cloud was focused on operations, efficiency, and cost-savings. Not completely sure of the decisions to continue laying people off when there is so much work to do which stretches remaining employees even more.
advice_to_management: Stop layoffs, both larger and quieter ones. Communicate like real people instead of in corp-speak. Refocus on the user instead of on AI. Stop doing stock buybacks and invest the money into retaining employees.

title: Feels like Amazon
pros: Employed, but spending 80% of earnings due to cost of living
cons: Unfortunately Ive ended up on an account with toxic and highly insecure extended team (specifically CAL, Account Executive and Principal Architect).  The exiting TAM's were burnt due to toxic culture of controlling nature and intentional sabotage by this team. Bay area googlers in general seem to constantly compare themselves with others even on personal front, with no sign of contentment. My only hope is my manager who is super helpful and I've been somehow blessed to find 3-4 TAM's from extended teams who are helpful but also burnt out. But being on this team isn't sustainable for my mental health and no amount of perks can make up for it. The stress is so high due to demanding nature, Ive already visited the urgent care in less then 3 months, and the day to day work is 80% manual entries due to lack of trust in tools. It feels like working a bank job in the 80's. I love technology and I genuinely believe in the helping others, but my smile has all but vanished at work because I'm always in fight or flight mode.
advice_to_management: Knowing the problem isnt enough,  you must give feedback and make change for your people. The avg life of a TAM on this account is 1 year, understandable so.

title: Not quite 'just another big company.' Yet. 3.5 stars.
pros: There's a lot of good stuff to work on, fantastic tooling, and less tech debt than most places of similar size. Colleagues are usually very skilled and smart.
cons:
There was a time when Google had an unusually good culture of openness, technical excellence, doing the right thing, and not letting all the usual bureaucratic turf wars bog it down. That time has entirely passed.
Getting to work on the good stuff is a matter of politics over suitability, performance reviews have gained the bad parts of the standard methods and kept the bad parts of the idiosyncratic methods, and you *will* face turf wars that get in the way of doing your job.
Also the strategy to avoid tech debt is 'reimplement everything after 5 years or kill it' which has always had its drawbacks.
advice_to_management: If you actually care? Beg Larry and Sergey back and give them veto on all top-level execs. Maybe someone else they pick to exercise the veto. Nothing less will be enough to restore the good times; the culture rotted from the top down starting as they left.

title: Strong growth environment with high expectations
pros: Smart and driven team that cares about results. Clear focus on product impact and measurable outcomes. You get real ownership and visibility from day one. Fast decision making and low bureaucracy. Leadership is accessible and open to discussion. Good place to grow if you take initiative and handle pressure well.
cons: Pace is intense and priorities shift often. Work life balance depends on your team and current goals. Processes are still evolving, which creates ambiguity. Expectations are high and feedback can be very direct. Not ideal if you prefer highly structured environments.
advice_to_management: Invest more in long term planning and clearer prioritization. Reduce context switching so teams can focus on fewer, higher impact bets. Continue improving internal communication around strategy changes. Formalize career progression paths to retain strong performers.

title: Good but getting worse
pros:
- Great WLB for most teams (except for Google Cloud)
- Great salary and benefits
- Have the ability to ship features that millions will use
- Invests in junior developers
- If you get bored, there are many teams to switch to if you live in a major tech hub
cons:
- Your experience can be very team dependent
- Hard to get promoted past L4/L5. You have to constantly prove your work has "impact". This is difficult if your team does not work on something "impactful". There is some politics involved here.
- Increasing layoffs and fear about layoffs
- Focus on AI means other projects and teams lose resources
- Classic big company bureaucracy
- Anything you want to do needs a document (doc). Need to develop a feature: design doc (makes sense). But wait, before you write that doc, you need a doc about the doc (Huh?). But now there are too many docs, you need a doc that summarizes both the docs and links to both docs. Obviously I'm exaggerating but you will spend more time writing docs that nobody will read than writing the actual 50 line code change.
- Engineers can be incentivized to make needlessly complex implementations of features because it makes them look smarter
- More stringent about hybrid work (tracking badge ins to the office)
- Google getting involved in politics and military contracts (Project Nimbus)
- Google stopped DEI initiatives

title: Big tech, comes with perks but has it's cons too
pros:
latest and greatest technology
access to alot of resources
benefits and perks
good team dynamic and partnership with hiring leaders
emphasis on work/life balance
diverse culture
cons:
large org structure - layers of skip levels to get to leadership
alot of red tape and robust processes - makes it difficult to make an immediate impact or enact change
some teams tend to be cliques - often resulted in promos for those that had better relationships rather than the quality of their work
advice_to_management:
focus on quality of work and not who has the best friendship/relationship with you 
I don't agree with the notion that "we are a big family", we are not we are coworkers, colleageus, and partners and should be focused on that in a high performing environment

title: Strong design culture and impact, but limited stability for contractors
pros:
• Exposure to complex, high-impact problems at massive scale  
• Strong design culture with thoughtful peers and clear craft standards  
• Opportunity to work closely with engineers and PMs on real production systems  
• Access to high-quality tools, data, and user research infrastructure  
• Projects that look meaningful and credible on a design portfolio
cons:
• Contractor experience can feel isolating compared to full-time employees  
• Limited visibility into long-term strategy and career growth  
• Contract roles often lack stability and clear conversion paths  
• Decision-making can be slow due to size and cross-org dependencies  
• Impact may depend heavily on team, manager, and org maturity

title: BAD ENVIRONMENT
pros: I want to join Aarki because it combines AI-driven mobile advertising, real-time programmatic, and performance analytics in a privacy-first environment. I enjoy using data to directly impact campaign performance and client growth. Aarki’s focus on context signals, audience modeling, and end-to-end optimization aligns well with my analytical background and career goals.
cons: First, I identify issues: duplicates, nulls, timestamp errors, or tracking gaps. I filter or remove invalid records, use consistent time zones, and deduplicate at device/ID level. I document data rules and validate with aggregate checks (total impressions, spend, conversions). If data is incomplete, I note limitations and use conservative estimates or subset analysis.

cons: When stock is down, the company stops caring about your well-being. It's becoming more of a typical corporation - caring about profits over people, encouraging less work-life balance, a lot less perks, a 'hustle' culture where if you're not constantly promoting yourself or trying to get promoted they will try to boot you out and generally a shift in the culture that many other employees and former employees I've spoken to describe as toxic.
advice_to_management: Don't fall into the toxic culture trap, stop being afraid to speak up when leadership is pushing things that are against the core values that Google grew up with and pushed for 10-15 years.

title: Strong compensation, world-class peers, but pace and ambiguity aren’t for everyone
pros:
Compensation and benefits are among the best in the industry, especially once you factor in equity and refreshers.

You work with very smart, driven engineers; code quality expectations are high.

Strong internal tooling, documentation, and infra—most problems already have a scalable solution somewhere.

Generally good work–life balance compared to other big tech, depending heavily on team.

Brand name opens doors later in your career.
cons:
Impact can feel diluted. Projects are large, and individual contributions may take time to show results.

Internal processes can be slow and bureaucratic; getting consensus often matters as much as technical merit.

Performance reviews and promotion criteria can feel opaque and political, especially at mid to senior levels.

Team experience varies widely - your day-to-day quality of life depends a lot on your manager and org.

Frequent reorgs can derail long-term work.

title: nnovative environment with brilliant peers, but growth can feel unstructured
pros:
Cutting-edge projects: You get to work on products used by billions. The scale and impact are unmatched.
Brilliant colleagues: The hiring bar is high, so you’re surrounded by smart, passionate people who push you to grow.
Outstanding benefits: From free gourmet meals and onsite wellness centers to generous parental leave and 401(k) matching—the perks are industry-leading.
Flexibility & autonomy: Managers generally trust you to manage your time and projects. Hybrid/remote options are widely available post-pandemic.
Learning culture: Internal talks, workshops, and access to resources like Coursera keep you constantly learning.
cons:
Promotions can be opaque: Advancement often feels political or dependent on visibility rather than pure technical skill. The perf (performance review) process is stressful and sometimes arbitrary.
Growing bureaucracy: As Google scales, decision-making has slowed. More layers of approval and “alignment meetings” than in the past.
Team dependence: Your experience varies wildly by team. Some teams have great WLB, others expect late nights and weekends—especially in cloud or ads.
Competitive internal mobility: Transferring to a desired team/project can be surprisingly competitive and time-consuming.
Feeling like a cog: On larger products, it’s easy to feel removed from the end-user impact.

A lot of AI...
Some orgs are way more stressful than others, but at least you have job security :)
Perks at Work discounts suck, you basically get a 0.5% to 10% discount.
advice_to_management: There's too much push to increase productivity just because 'AI' can assist with it. There's been constant pressure to use all of these AI tools (and there's a ton and more in progress) and recent deadlines seem to take into account that there should be a substantial productivity increase.

title: Amazing company with great benefits
pros:
Very smart, collaborative coworkers; you learn fast just by being around the talent density.
Excellent compensation and benefits; strong internal mobility if you’re proactive.
High-quality engineering culture: solid tooling, code reviews, and emphasis on reliability at scale.
Interesting problems with real-world reach—your work can affect millions of users.
cons:
Cons:
Ambiguity and shifting priorities can be common; you may need to drive clarity yourself.
Large-company overhead (process, alignment, reviews) can slow execution compared to startups.
Work-life balance varies a lot by org/team; some groups are intense and deadline-driven.
It can take time to build influence and get things shipped in a big system.

title: Could be great for many people - wasn't for me
pros: Great pay and benefits. Office full to the brim with amenities. Despite tracking badge swipes to enforce hybrid work policies, my manager and team was very flexible. Work/life balance was some of the best I've experienced in ad tech. If you get your work done, no one cares very much where you are.
cons:
I can only speak to the Google Ads/DV360 side, but I felt extremely isolated. Virtually everyone that I worked directly with was distributed across the country, which felt at odds with the hybrid, 3 days a week in-office schedule. The people I sat alongside who were in my same role (or adjacent roles) were nice, but it didn't outweigh the fact that I was commuting just to send emails to colleagues and clients in other time zones. If I'm going to be in an office, I value community, and instead I felt totally anonymous. Nobody cared when I came and left which, while beneficial for doctor's appointments and the like, got old really quickly. I might as well have not been there.

Additionally, I felt the culture prioritized and rewarded tenure above all. Granted, I recognize I was in a difficult position as the first new hire for my team in a few years. They had gone through rounds of layoffs in years prior. The next newest person on my team after me had at least 3-4 years of Google experience, and most on my team were nearing 7-10+ years. In my experience, the greater the tenure, the more people felt like they could talk down to you--never explicitly, but implicitly. I regularly had Account Executives on my team dictating emails for me to send to clients, completely overhauling decks I spend multiple days on, not looping me into important conversations, etc. It was as though I had no work experience prior to Google, like I just walked through those doors having never done any client-facing work. As if Google is some particularly special place--it didn't feel that special to me. I'm sure many people would enjoy this type of environment, but I did not.

title: Great place to work
pros: Working at Google offers an exceptional opportunity to collaborate with brilliant minds on massive-scale technical challenges while enjoying industry-leading benefits and campus amenities. However, navigating the bureaucracy of such a large organization can be slow, making it difficult at times to see the direct impact of your individual contributions on final products.
cons: The sheer size of the organization often leads to sluggish bureaucracy and "red tape," making it difficult for individuals to ship products quickly or feel a strong sense of ownership. Furthermore, the internal competition for promotions and the frequent cancellation of projects can make career progression feel more political than merit-based.

title: NEVER WORK HERE if I could give 0 stars I would
pros: The only pros were my direct management and the ability to flex
cons: Pay, benefits, insurance, higher leadership
advice_to_management: TAKE CARE OF THE EMPLOYEES WHO MAKE A PROFIT FOR YOUR COMPANY. Terrible pay, terrible insurance, terrible leadership, terrible policies. I worked for this company for two years. Never missed a day of work, never clocked in late, barely took PTO, never had even a point in the entirety of employment. Provided a two weeks notice as this is customary when you don't want to drown your team in additional work and the company has time to problem solve for departure. When I should have just never given a notice and left, because they took my EARNED NON DISCRETIONARY BONUS due to me given notice 5 days prior to the bonus being billed. When I asked if I could have a copy of the policy, none was provided. This company continuously overloads Insurance verification team and treats it like a catch all dumpster management offers very little help, with the exception of Bethany who goes above and beyond for a company that continues to beat her down. The workload does not even match compensation. I worked there for two years and received an .82 cent raise and was always in good standings. Their maternity leave is 4 weeks. FOUR WEEKs so god forbid you have to have a C-section because you're only getting that 4 weeks paid. Insurance is terrible. Benefits are terrible. The lack of support from high leadership is disgusting. You are a body to this company nothing more. Please save your time and apply elsewhere.

cons: Google's infra and software stack complicates a lot of tasks, which can be discouraging and if you're the kind of person who is easily affected by this kind of friction it may be wise to consider another opportunity, if available. I found that it was hard to maintain velocity. Sometimes it was the company's fault, sometimes it was my fault, but at the end of the day it ended up not being the right place for me long-term.

title: Flexible, intellectually stimulating freelance role
pros:
Flexible remote schedule and interesting math problems.

Collaborative team, clear communication, fair project scope.

Good pay for freelance contributions; timely payments.

Opportunities to improve problem-solving and pedagogy.
cons:
Occasional tight deadlines with limited advance notice.

Scope changes can happen mid-project unexpectedly.

Limited long-term benefits or full-time perks.

Some tasks require extra QA time.
advice_to_management:
Give earlier notice for deadline changes.

Improve scope documentation before projects begin.

Consider repeat-hire pathways for top freelancers.

title: Great Pay and Benefits, Poor Leadership & Culture
pros: Salary, bonus and equity. Amazing insurance through Anthem with Included Health.
cons: This is not the Google of 2019. The leadership, especially on the People/HR side, is obsessive about lowering cost by moving HR services to AI products and farming out the rest to incompetent vendors. Many at the company live in constant fear of layoffs. Prior focus on flexibility and work-life balance is all but gone; Options for remote work removed and programs like Work from Anywhere Weeks are being punitively revamped to minimize use. Options for promo are few and distanced. All DEI programs have been sunset.
advice_to_management: Replace leadership on the People Operations side; they have lost the confidence of your employees and are cultivating a culture of fear. In the last 2 years of meetings lead by Tom, John and/or Fiona, the emphasis has only been on return to office, Gemini and constant ineffective reorgs. There is no mention of actual people-first programs or empathy, which is why many that report up this line are in the field to begin with.

Managers call their reportees in the middle of the night and during weekends on their cell phone. Meetings that are supposed to be an hour long, frequently continue for 2-2.5 hrs on demand of any of the senior leaders. Barely any break during the day to even eat or go to the washroom. Working for any company is better than working for Google today if you want to have a life outside work. This is specific to Google cloud.
advice_to_management: Wake up before all the good talent find some place else to work.

title: Great place to work. Great benefits. Could share more profits after a $100B year. Executive leadership detached/unaware.
pros:
From the perspective of the data center teams:
- Tons of great, caring people to work with
- Some great managers
- Opportunities and career paths
- THE best engineering talent in the industry. (Fight me. 🤣)
- Excellent benefits/perks
- Stock grants/RSUs, Great stock outlook
- AMAZING tech to work with/on and constant, new platforms
- Mind-blowing scale
- Occasional travel opportunities
- More later as I think of it...
cons:
From the perspective of the data center teams:
- Google laid off 12,000 employees in January of 2023 without any warning to managers or even regional directors. (All local management was caught completely off-guard that morning until they started getting phone calls from employees wondering just what the hell the email meant and what the hell was going on...leaving management in a REALLY crappy position without any ability whatsoever to answer. That was a SUPER horrible thing to do to your leadership teams.)

This event had a HEAVY impact on morale and SCARED everyone who was left. It left us in a grave sense of uncertainty; enhanced by the way they did it so suddenly and without warning or explanation.

But, possibly, the worst part... We saw MANY amazing employees who were well-respect and very knowledgable end up being exiled from their DREAM JOB. It was so INCREDIBLY SAD to see. It angered us. And there was no real BELIEVABLE statement from Google given to us to recognize that this was a very real problem. (Local management also felt the same burn as the rest of us, though. WE took care of EACH OTHER.)

- ZERO explanation from executive leadership regarding the criteria that they used to select who was laid off and who wasn't (Still a mystery as of Dec2025)

- Since the layoffs, executive leadership has forced local management into a position where metrics matter more than the people and, in order to deliver, they have been forced to turn to EXTREME micro-management...which is counterintuitive to employee mental well-being

-  Since the layoffs executive leadership seems completely unaware of how things actually work "down in the trenches" of the data centers and try to dictate policy based on that lack of understanding. (Which means that they tend to ignore much of what local leadership tries to convey to them.)

- Google has since been "tightening the belt" - so to speak - and sometimes acting like certain things that are well within reason are "not a priority
advice_to_management:
EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP: Start trying to remember that the people who report to you...and ALL of the people who report them THEM - down to the youngest, most inexperienced tech in the data centers - have their OWN goals, stresses and concerns. STOP trying to push such an accelerated agenda downward that some of those people get lost in the process. Go and take a few WEEKS... Find one of the data centers... Go LEARN the job. DO the job. Get to understanding the amount of energy and effort it takes for us to deliver what you ask. (i.e. Walk some miles in our shoes... Almost LITERALLY.) Learn to have REAL, PERSONAL appreciation for that work, energy and effort. Learn how TRULY dedicated we are...and that it's often true that we are SO DEDICATED to what we do that it is to our own detriment. We burn ourselves out sometimes and it doesn't feel like, since the layoffs, you really have the kind of appreciation that we in the trenches deserve.

You take orders and expectations from VP leadership above. Yes. But you should ALSO be "managing upward" and advocating for US more than it seems like you do.

LOCAL LEADERSHIP: You guys are AMAZING. Many of us know that you guys work SUPER HARD to help and support us. Some of you are new. Some of you are still learning and figuring things out since you were hired recently. But there are still a few among you that REALLY ought to consider finding roles elsewhere because you're just not effective and/or you're too checked-out to care about your reports. Be real with yourselves and figure it out. Either re-engage...or LEAVE so that those reports can get on new teams with different managers who WILL care for and support them.

Otherwise... MOST of you are AWESOME and really do your best to make sure that we have support and that we're happy...while ALSO striving to meet the business needs of the company.

Otherwise? See the "Cons" section for more.

title: Amazing people, Difficult to challenge status quo
pros: If you are in the right place, it all works great.
cons:
If you are in the wrong team it is a struggle. The political set up is such that you need the seniors to help you move up. You need to be good, and also be a "good boy". Depending on the group, sometimes, you can be a nothing and still expect to get rewarded, though I hope what I saw happen is an exception. 

Trying to challenge and work your way up is very very hard. You need to be very lucky.
advice_to_management: The system is really rotten at places but everyone believes they are great. Pretty hard to clean up. "Average" leaders, especially at senior level, should never be accepted. Otherwise, there is no hope for improvement.

title: Great company to work for
pros: You can work a good chunk of your time and most teams on something that you are interested in. They officially got rid of the 20% project but you can get unofficial approval to do it from your manager for getting your work done. Most people are able to do this. The sushi at the Sunnyvale TC one office is dank.
cons: If you move further up the engineering ladder, they start to want you to mentor people and stuff scheduling and context switching between talking to people and doing actual engineering work can be challenging for some people, including myself. I wouldn’t call this a dealbreaker. I would just say that you need to schedule “do not schedule” sections in your calendar if you plan to do technical work.

title: It's gone way down hill
